title: Fixing the Skills Mismatch: What's needed now
gist: Jobs for the Future CEO Maria Flynn argues the core labour-market problem is a skills mismatch, not a talent shortage, and that legacy education/workforce systems can't keep pace with AI. She predicts AI will force adoption of skills-based hiring and rapid, continuous reskilling, while worker sentiment toward AI is souring in an uneven 'messy middle.'

insights.claim: The biggest constraint on labour markets is not a talent shortage but a skills mismatch: organizations struggle to match talent to roles rather than with the supply of workers itself.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Reframes the labour-market bottleneck away from headcount toward matching and assessment, favoring skills-assessment tooling and HR-tech over pure recruiting supply plays.
insights.quote: one of the biggest constraints is the skill mismatch
insights.locator: According to our recent Chief People Officer Outlook

insights.claim: Education and public workforce systems were built for a different era and are not agile enough to keep pace with AI-driven labour-market change, risking a 'divorce' of supply and demand.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Signals structural, policy-level opportunity for workforce-reform and reskilling providers that can move faster than incumbent public systems.
insights.quote: we run the risk of divorcing supply and demand because the systems aren't agile enough to keep pace
insights.locator: Our education systems, our public workforce systems

insights.claim: Fixing the mismatch requires a combination of policy change, practice change, and mindset change rather than any single lever.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Investors and policy-watchers should treat culture/mindset shift as the binding constraint, not just funding or regulation.
insights.quote: the solves for this are a combination of policy change, practice changes and also mindset changes
insights.locator: So really, we run the risk

insights.claim: The reskilling era ahead will look fundamentally different from post-2008: less about earning a new 6-18 month credential and more about rapid, continuous learning of new skills, often through AI-enhanced learning.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Bearish for slow, credential-based retraining models; bullish for rapid, AI-native, just-in-time upskilling platforms.
insights.quote: This is going be less about someone going back to get a new credential over the course of six or 12 or 18 months. It's going to be much more about rapid learning of new skills.
insights.locator: I think this era of reskilling and upskilling

insights.claim: Jobs for the Future has set a goal of 75 million quality jobs in the U.S. by 2033, roughly doubling the number of workers in quality jobs today.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: A concrete target that anchors the scale of the workforce-development market the organization is trying to build.
insights.quote: 75 million quality jobs by 2033. That's doubling the number of quality jobs that workers are in today.
insights.locator: It also has one great big goal

insights.claim: A 'quality job' has five essential dimensions: pay and benefits, ability to advance in a career, worker agency/voice, a safe and respectful workplace, and access to and some control over one's schedule.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: A standardized job-quality taxonomy that HR, ESG raters, and impact investors can use to benchmark employers beyond wages.
insights.quote: a quality job we believe has five essential elements
insights.locator: So, a quality job we believe

insights.claim: The optimal mix of job-quality dimensions varies by individual and shifts across a person's career, so job quality should be assessed as a multi-dimensional trade-off rather than a single score.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Suggests personalization of benefits and scheduling as a retention lever rather than one-size-fits-all packages.
insights.quote: the right mix of those dimensions may vary and the mix of dimensions might vary across their career
insights.locator: We realized that for every individual

insights.claim: Only about 25% of workers currently have what would be considered quality scheduling policies, and high-quality scheduling correlates strongly with job satisfaction and stronger retention.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Scheduling is a large, cheap, under-exploited retention lever; workforce-management and scheduling software has room to reduce costly turnover.
insights.quote: only about 25% of workers right now have what we would consider to be kind of quality scheduling policies
insights.locator: Yeah, so I think scheduling is another piece

insights.claim: Many practices that lift worker satisfaction, such as giving employees a voice and transparency around safety and scheduling, cost employers little money.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Low-capex retention levers imply satisfaction and turnover gains achievable without margin-eroding pay hikes.
insights.quote: there are a lot of practices that employers can put into place that don't cost a lot of money, but can really enhance worker satisfaction
insights.locator: So, I think it's easy things

themes.theme: AI worker sentiment and the messy middle

insights.claim: In Jobs for the Future's March 2026 survey, the share of people who feel AI is doing more harm than good reversed from the prior year, when sentiment had been net positive, indicating rising worker scepticism even as AI advances.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Souring worker sentiment is a leading indicator of adoption friction and potential backlash risk for AI-deploying employers.
insights.quote: one thing that surprised me is a shift in the percentage of people who feel that AI is doing more harm than good
insights.locator: we released our recent findings earlier this year, March 2026

insights.claim: Worker experience of AI is splitting roughly 50/50 across multiple axes, including whether they think they'll need new skills and whether they feel more or less connected, marking a 'messy middle' of uneven AI implementation.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: The bimodal response means AI rollout strategies must segment workers rather than assume uniform readiness or resistance.
insights.quote: we're very much in the messy middle of AI implementation
insights.locator: We're also seeing a lot of almost splits down the middle

insights.claim: Over 60% of workers say they wish they had more say in how technology, including AI, is deployed within their company.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Building authentic worker-input channels into AI rollouts is cheap and may raise both AI uptake and retention; watch for tools that operationalize worker voice.
insights.quote: many workers, over 60%, are saying that they wish they had more say in how technology, including artificial intelligence, is being deployed within their company
insights.locator: the one pain point that we're seeing

insights.claim: Embedding authentic worker feedback into AI adoption is expected to increase both worker satisfaction and the actual uptake of AI within companies, improving retention.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Ties change-management quality directly to AI ROI; poorly consulted rollouts may see lower tool adoption.
insights.quote: we think we'll see more uptake in the use of AI within those companies
insights.locator: So, that's kind of an easy thing that employers can do

insights.claim: AI disruption is concentrated at early-career levels, making advancement opportunities and skill investment for junior workers especially urgent.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Entry-level roles are the front line of AI displacement; watch entry-level hiring volumes and internal mobility programs as signals.
insights.quote: we're seeing such disruption, particularly at early-career levels
insights.locator: particularly in this age of AI

themes.theme: Equity gaps and the AI gender divide

insights.claim: Jobs for the Future prioritizes four worker groups in assessing AI impact: workers without four-year degrees, women, workers of colour, and workers impacted by the criminal justice system.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Defines the target population for equity-focused workforce products and impact-investing theses.
insights.locator: there are four big buckets of workers that we really prioritize

insights.claim: Recent findings show women are engaging with AI less than men and expressing more scepticism or fear, opening a concerning gender gap in both AI usage and AI leadership.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: A widening AI gender gap is a second-order risk for equitable adoption and a market for targeted upskilling of women.
insights.quote: we are not seeing as many women as men really engaging in AI
insights.locator: one group that really stood out in our recent findings was women

insights.claim: Gender-specific coding programs like Girls Who Code succeeded at getting women to complete training and get employed, but retention dropped once women were in employment, so belonging post-placement is the unsolved gap.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Placement metrics overstate success; retention/belonging interventions after hiring are the underserved need in diversity pipelines.
insights.quote: we saw a lot of retention issues once the individual was in employment
insights.locator: During the boot camp era

insights.claim: Proven equity levers include intentional inclusive program design, turning gateway college courses into pulling-in rather than screening-out mechanisms, cohort-based learning, and early career awareness in middle and high school.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Concrete, evidence-based design patterns for ed-tech and workforce programs seeking better diverse completion and retention.
insights.quote: How do we make those more inclusive, instead of a screening-out mechanism, really a pulling-in mechanism.
insights.locator: So one across the board is intentionality

insights.claim: Skills-based hiring, hiring on the basis of demonstrated skills rather than degrees held, is still in early days but is a promising approach to diversifying the workforce.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Structural shift away from degree-gating expands the addressable talent pool and demand for skills-assessment infrastructure.
insights.quote: hiring on the basis of skills that people have versus the degrees that they do or don't have
insights.locator: we are big proponents of skills-based talent practices

insights.claim: Jobs for the Future advocates 'skills-first' practices across the entire talent lifecycle, including advancement and manager assessments, not just at the hiring stage.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Expands the skills-tech opportunity from recruiting into internal mobility, performance, and manager tooling.
insights.quote: how can we be advancing folks in the workforce based on the skills that they have?
insights.locator: We would really advocate going beyond that

insights.claim: Jobs for the Future's venture arm, JFF Ventures, has invested in a tech platform called Adept ID that gives employers a common language and protocol to identify occupation-critical skills and assess workers against them beyond self-reporting.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Names a specific investable skills-assessment company and the strategic thesis behind its venture arm.
insights.quote: There's one that we invest in in our venture arm, JFA Ventures, called Adept ID
insights.locator: So, there are a lot of great tech platforms out there

insights.claim: The main barrier to scaling skills-based hiring is culture and mindset among hiring managers: almost all CEOs favour it, but reception is markedly weaker at the hiring-manager level.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Executive endorsement overstates real adoption; the deployment gap sits with middle-management, where change tools should target.
insights.quote: almost all CEOs will say they're in favour of it, but then when you get down to kind of hiring managers, there's less positive reception
insights.locator: But where we're seeing some of the roadblocks

insights.claim: The majority of U.S. states have removed degree requirements from state jobs, an example of policy change already driving skills-based hiring toward scale.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Public-sector degree deregulation is a concrete tailwind and template for private-sector adoption.
insights.quote: the majority of states have removed degree requirements from state jobs
insights.locator: some good things we are seeing

insights.claim: As AI accelerates, skills will become the 'currency' of the workforce, displacing credentials like MBAs or graduate degrees as the primary signal of what a worker can do.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Long-term devaluation of credential signaling strengthens the case for granular skills-taxonomy and assessment vendors.
insights.quote: it's also gonna make it more important for employers to be thinking about skills as the currency of their workforce
insights.locator: But we also, I think, given how quickly these changes are coming

themes.theme: Human/durable skills in the AI age

insights.claim: Human skills such as communication, problem solving, teamwork, and learning across differences will be the key to success in the age of AI, but schools and employers teach and assess these 'durable skills' poorly and unevenly.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
entities: Secretary's Commission on Achieving Necessary Skills
insights.so_what: A durable-skills assessment gap is a market opening; measurement of soft skills becomes an advancement mechanism worth building.
insights.quote: the humanity in our roles and in our work life are going to be what is key kind of to success in the age of AI
insights.locator: human skills are going to kind of go out of fashion

insights.claim: A 1990s federal commission (the Secretary's Commission on Achieving Necessary Skills) already named communication, problem solving, and teamwork as the key future skills, and re-running it today would yield nearly the same answer, showing the problem is teaching and assessment, not identification.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
entities: Secretary's Commission on Achieving Necessary Skills
insights.so_what: The bottleneck is embedding and measuring known skills, not discovering new ones; solutions should target pedagogy and assessment.
insights.quote: if we re-ran that report today, it would say pretty much the same thing
insights.locator: there was a federal commission called the Secretary's Commission

insights.claim: Employers will increasingly need to act as educators, teaching not just AI tools but people-to-people collaboration and durable skills.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Shifts training burden onto employers, expanding corporate L&D and enterprise learning spend.
insights.locator: employers will need to be educators

insights.claim: A four-day work week will not be adopted at scale anytime soon because workplace reforms take a long time to take hold and the conditions are not right.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for near-term structural work-time change; discount narratives betting on rapid four-day-week adoption.
insights.quote: I don't think that a four-day work week is one that we are going to see adopted at scale anytime soon
insights.locator: we'll finally get a four-day work week

insights.claim: The claim that white-collar jobs are over and everyone should become a tradesperson is rejected; skilled trades are vital and need more workers, but a rich mix of high-quality white- and blue-collar jobs will persist.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the 'learn to plumb' narrative; robust career-navigation systems, not wholesale sector migration, are the answer.
insights.quote: I don't think that means that we all need to be going down that path
insights.locator: The white-collar job is over and we should all become plumbers?

insights.claim: Specialists will have the advantage in the AI era: AI makes baseline general knowledge cheap and accessible, so those who augment that generalist base with specialized skills and analytics will be most in demand.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: AI commoditizes generalist knowledge; value accrues to specialized augmentation, a signal for skills-premium and niche-expertise plays.
insights.quote: I think AI is going to make it easier for everyone to be generalists in some way.
insights.locator: Specialists, not generalists, will be the kings

insights.claim: The four-year degree is not yet obsolete but will have to change: universities must embrace experiential and work-based learning and embed career navigation into core curriculum, becoming one of many high-quality options.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Pressure on higher-ed business models to add work-based learning; ed-tech enabling experiential learning gains relevance.
insights.quote: They're going to have be embracing more experiential learning and work-based learning opportunities
insights.locator: The four-year degree is obsolete. Yes or no?

insights.claim: Liberal arts majors will not 'step into their power' unless paired with strong labour-market knowledge and real-world application, or they risk remaining too traditional and 'ivory towerish' for today's economy.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Value of humanities credentials is conditional on applied, market-aware framing, relevant to curriculum-design and career-services bets.
insights.quote: they're going to continue to be a little too traditional and ivory towerish and not as agile and real world as we need
insights.locator: Liberal arts majors will finally step into their power

insights.claim: The skills mismatch will finally be fixed because the speed of AI advancement will force supply and demand to be reconnected, making the fix imperative rather than optional.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Frames AI as the forcing function that accelerates workforce-reform adoption, a tailwind for skills-infrastructure providers.
insights.quote: We are gonna finally fix the skills mismatch because advances in AI are going to force us to.
insights.locator: We will finally fix the skills mismatch

insights.claim: Individual workers, and students, should proactively lean into AI now, actively building awareness and skills, because AI will increasingly be a reality on the job over the coming months and years.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: AI fluency is becoming table stakes; individual upskilling demand and self-directed learning tools should grow.
insights.quote: They should be open to AI. They should be taking steps to learn as much as they can
insights.locator: What's one thing they should be doing now?

insights.claim: Career advancement should be reframed as lifelong learning-and-earning that ebbs and flows across a lifetime, replacing the traditional one-and-done model of school then work.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Lifelong, non-linear learning-earning models expand recurring demand for reskilling and career-navigation services.
insights.quote: a true lifelong learning model, not like a traditional one-and-done approach where you go to school and then you go to work
insights.locator: Ten years from now, if we are able to have our systems

top_entities: Secretary's Commission on Achieving Necessary Skills
